enrichment.short_description: Resource management and capacity planning software that helps agencies and professional services teams schedule people, track utilization, and forecast project financials in real time.
enrichment.pricing_model: Per-resource seat subscription, minimum 20 seats, monthly or annual billing, with a free trial
enrichment.seo_title: Runn Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: An independent look at Runn's resource planning software, covering seat-based pricing, scheduling and forecasting features, and who it is really built for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Runn is a resource management and capacity planning platform founded in 2018 and headquartered in Wellington, New Zealand. The company was built by a remote-first team to replace spreadsheet-based resourcing with a real-time view of who is working on what, helping agencies, consultancies, and other professional services organizations balance workload, forecast utilization, and protect project margins. As of 2026 Runn reports serving 500-plus customers and over 15,000 users across more than 100 countries, with its platform having tracked more than 47 million logged hours, over 53,000 planned projects, and roughly 38,000 resource allocations, figures the company publishes on its own site as usage milestones rather than third-party audited statistics. Runn's core product centers on a visual scheduling grid that shows tentative and confirmed project assignments alongside capacity and availability charts, so managers can spot overbooking or bench time before it becomes a problem. It layers in project financials, letting teams model budgets, billable rates, and forecasted revenue against actual time logged, and it supports skills and tags so resourcing decisions can be filtered by who is qualified for a given role. Runn prices itself on a per-resource seat basis rather than per named user, meaning every person placed on the planner, whether or not they log into the software, counts toward the seat total, and plans start at a 20-resource minimum. The Lite plan is $7 per resource per month and includes scheduling, tentative projects, capacity charts, skills and tags, and project financials. The Standard plan is $11 per resource per month and adds timesheets, five custom fields, third-party integrations, API access, and a 30-day audit log. Runn also offers a free trial that does not require a credit card, letting prospective customers test the scheduling and forecasting workflow before committing to a paid seat count. The product integrates with common project and finance tools and is aimed squarely at teams that need to reconcile people-planning with financial forecasting in one place, distinguishing it from pure timesheet tools or generic project management software. Because pricing scales with headcount rather than software users, Runn tends to suit mid-sized professional services firms with dozens to low hundreds of billable staff rather than very small teams or large enterprises with thousands of resources, where seat costs would compound quickly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Runn is a resource management and capacity planning tool built for agencies, consultancies, and other professional services businesses that need to schedule people across multiple concurrent projects. Founded in 2018 and based in Wellington, New Zealand, the company positions Runn as a replacement for spreadsheet-based resourcing, combining a visual scheduling grid with project financial forecasting so managers can see workload and budget impact together instead of in separate tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Runn's scheduling grid shows confirmed and tentative project assignments alongside capacity and availability charts, making it easy to identify overbooked staff or unused bench time at a glance. Skills and tags let planners filter resourcing decisions by role or qualification, while project financials track budgets, billable rates, and forecasted revenue against logged time. The Standard plan adds timesheets, custom fields, integrations, API access, and an audit log for teams that need tighter reporting and compliance controls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Runn uses resource seat-based pricing rather than per-user licensing, meaning every person placed on the planner counts toward the seat total regardless of whether they log in. Plans start at a 20-resource minimum, with the Lite plan priced at $7 per resource per month and the Standard plan at $11 per resource per month. A free trial without a required credit card is available for teams that want to test the workflow before purchasing seats.

features.description: A drag-and-drop planner grid shows confirmed and tentative assignments across every project so managers can spot conflicts before they happen.

features.description: Tag people by role, skill, or qualification so resourcing decisions can be filtered to the right fit for a project.

features.title: Timesheets
features.description: Available on the Standard plan, timesheets let teams reconcile planned hours against actual time worked.

features.title: Custom fields and API access
features.description: Standard plan customers can add custom fields and connect Runn to other systems through the API and integrations.

features.title: Audit log
features.description: A 30-day audit log on the Standard plan gives visibility into changes made across the planner for accountability.

features.title: Multi-project, multi-team view
features.description: Runn is designed for organizations juggling many concurrent projects and teams rather than single-project tracking.

pros: Resource-seat pricing scales predictably with headcount rather than software logins
Combines scheduling and financial forecasting in one visual tool instead of separate spreadsheets
Free trial available without requiring a credit card
Skills and tags make it easy to match the right person to the right project
Remote-first company with a global customer base across 100-plus countries
cons: Seat-based pricing counts every planned resource, which can raise costs for larger teams even if they rarely log in
20-resource minimum makes it less economical for very small teams
Timesheets, integrations, and API access are locked behind the higher Standard tier
Company size is relatively small, which may mean slower feature velocity than larger competitors
Primarily built for professional services scheduling, so it is less suited to general project management needs

enrichment.short_description: A GPU cloud platform for AI and machine learning workloads, offering on-demand Pods, autoscaling serverless inference, and multi-node clusters billed by the second.
enrichment.pricing_model: Usage-based, pay-per-second GPU billing with no subscription requirement
enrichment.starting_price: From around $0.24 per GPU-hour depending on GPU type and cloud tier
enrichment.headquarters: Moorestown, New Jersey, United States (remote-first team)
enrichment.seo_title: RunPod Review 2026: GPU Cloud Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: How RunPod's per-second GPU cloud pricing works across Pods, Serverless, and Clusters, plus founding facts and who the platform is built for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RunPod is a GPU cloud computing platform built for AI and machine learning workloads, founded on October 31, 2022 by Zhen Lu (CEO) and Pardeep Singh (CTO). The company is incorporated in Moorestown, New Jersey, and operates as a remote-first team distributed across the United States, Canada, Europe, and India rather than from a single physical headquarters. By early 2026 RunPod had grown to roughly 98 to 101 employees, a lean team relative to a platform reported to serve more than 750,000 developers in 183 countries, and third-party estimates have placed its run-rate revenue in the neighborhood of $120 million ARR. RunPod's core offering is split across three product lines. Pods provide dedicated GPU instances billed per hour for development work and long-running training or fine-tuning jobs, available through both a lower-cost Community Cloud tier and a more isolated Secure Cloud tier; example on-demand rates include an H100 SXM around $2.99 per hour, an A100 PCIe around $1.39 per hour, and an RTX 4090 around $0.69 per hour. Serverless is built for inference workloads, autoscaling GPU workers up and down based on API traffic and billing only for active usage, with example rates such as an H100 around $4.55 per hour, an A100 around $2.72 per hour, and an L4 around $0.69 per hour. Clusters support multi-node, multi-GPU deployments for distributed training, with per-hour pricing such as an H200 SXM around $4.31 per hour and an A100 SXM around $1.79 per hour, plus volume discounts for reserved capacity at scale. Across all products RunPod bills by the second rather than rounding up to the hour, and it advertises no minimum commitments and no egress fees, positioning itself as up to 80 percent cheaper than large hyperscale clouds for comparable GPU capacity. Storage is priced separately, with container disk around $0.10 per GB per month and network storage ranging from about $0.05 to $0.07 per GB per month for standard tiers up to $0.14 per GB per month for high-performance storage. The platform targets developers, startups, and ML teams who need flexible, short-notice access to GPUs for training, fine-tuning, and inference without negotiating long-term cloud contracts, and it is generally not positioned as a general-purpose cloud provider outside of GPU-centric compute.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RunPod is a GPU cloud platform focused specifically on AI and machine learning workloads. Founded in October 2022 by Zhen Lu and Pardeep Singh, the company is incorporated in Moorestown, New Jersey, but runs as a remote-first team spread across the US, Canada, Europe, and India. RunPod has grown quickly into a developer-focused alternative to hyperscale clouds, reporting service to hundreds of thousands of developers across nearly 200 countries with a lean employee base.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RunPod splits its offering into Pods for dedicated GPU instances used in development and long-running jobs, Serverless for autoscaling inference workers billed only on active usage, and Clusters for multi-node distributed training. All three are billed per second with no minimum commitments and no egress fees, and Pods are further split into a lower-cost Community Cloud tier and a more isolated Secure Cloud tier depending on the sensitivity of the workload.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RunPod pricing is entirely usage-based rather than subscription-based, with rates varying by GPU model and product line. Example on-demand Pod rates include roughly $2.99 per hour for an H100 SXM and $0.69 per hour for an RTX 4090, while Serverless inference workers range from about $0.69 to $4.55 per hour depending on GPU class. Storage is billed separately, from about $0.05 per GB per month for standard network storage up to $0.14 per GB per month for high-performance storage.

features.title: Per-second GPU billing
features.description: Usage is billed down to the second rather than rounded to the hour, so short jobs only pay for time actually used.

features.title: Pods for dedicated instances
features.description: Dedicated GPU instances for development, training, and long-running jobs across Community and Secure Cloud tiers.

features.title: Serverless inference workers
features.description: Autoscaling GPU workers for inference workloads that scale to zero and bill only for active usage.

features.title: Multi-node Clusters
features.description: Reserved, multi-GPU cluster deployments for distributed training workloads at scale.

features.title: No egress fees
features.description: Data transfer out of RunPod is not charged separately, unlike many hyperscale cloud providers.

features.title: Wide GPU selection
features.description: Access to GPUs ranging from consumer-class RTX cards to data-center H100 and H200 accelerators.

features.title: Network and container storage
features.description: Separately priced persistent network storage and container disk options for model weights and data.

features.title: Global availability
features.description: Infrastructure reported to serve developers across 183 countries via a distributed data center footprint.

pros: Per-second billing with no minimum commitments keeps costs predictable for short jobs
Wide range of GPU types from consumer cards to H100 and H200 accelerators
No egress fees, unlike many hyperscale cloud providers
Serverless tier autoscales inference workers to zero when idle
Lean, remote-first team has scaled to serve a large global developer base
Significantly cheaper than large hyperscalers for comparable GPU capacity
cons: Community Cloud tier trades some security and reliability guarantees for lower price
Pricing spans multiple product lines, which can be confusing to compare at a glance
Specific GPU model availability can fluctuate with demand
Requires technical comfort with containers and APIs to get the most out of the platform
Support options are more limited without an enterprise-level relationship
Relatively young company founded in 2022, with less track record than legacy cloud providers

enrichment.short_description: An AI research and applied media company best known for its Gen-4 family of generative video and image models used by filmmakers, advertisers, and creative teams.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription with monthly credit allowances, billed monthly or annually
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan with 125 one-time credits; paid plans from $12 per month billed annually
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Runway Review 2026: Gen-4 AI Video Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What Runway's Gen-4 AI video and image platform costs, how its credit-based plans work, and who the tool suits, based on verified company facts.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Runway, legally Runway AI Inc. and commonly known by its product name Runway ML, is an AI research and applied media company founded in 2018 by Cristobal Valenzuela, Alejandro Matamala, and Anastasis Germanidis, who met while studying at New York University's Tisch School of the Arts ITP program. The company is headquartered in New York City, with additional offices reported in San Francisco, Seattle, London, and Tel Aviv, and operates largely remote-first across North America and Europe. By 2026 Runway had grown to roughly 480 to 500 employees according to workforce data providers, up sharply from its early startup days, and had raised a total of about $860 million across seven funding rounds, including a Series E round of $315 million closed in February 2026. Its valuation rose from about $3 billion in April 2025 to roughly $5.3 billion by February 2026, with revenue estimates cited around $300 million in annualized recurring revenue. Runway is best known for its Gen-4 family of generative AI models, which target character, location, and object consistency across scenes for professional film, advertising, and creative production, competing directly with offerings like Kling, Google's Veo, and Luma's Ray models. The platform also offers Gen-4.5 video generation, AI image generation using both Runway's own models and third-party models such as Nano Banana Pro, text-to-speech, and a browser-based video editor. Pricing runs on a freemium, credit-based model. The Free plan offers 125 one-time credits along with limited video editor projects and 5GB of asset storage, with generations carrying a watermark. The Standard plan costs $12 per month billed annually, or $15 per month billed monthly, and includes 625 credits per month, roughly 52 seconds of Gen-4.5 video, full access to Runway's AI image and video models, 4K upscaling, and no watermark. The Pro plan is $28 per month annually, or $35 monthly, with 2,250 credits per month, custom voice creation, and 500GB of storage. The Max plan is $76 per month annually, or $95 monthly, with 9,500 credits per month and credit rollover of up to one month. An Enterprise plan with custom credit packages, SSO, workspace analytics, and priority support is available for larger organizations. Runway targets professional and semi-professional creative users, from independent filmmakers to marketing and advertising teams, who need fast iteration on AI-generated video and image content rather than casual hobbyist use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Runway is a New York City-based AI research and applied media company founded in 2018 by Cristobal Valenzuela, Alejandro Matamala, and Anastasis Germanidis, who met at NYU's Tisch School of the Arts. The company is best known for its Gen-4 family of AI video and image generation models, which emphasize consistent characters, locations, and objects across scenes for professional film, advertising, and creative production.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Runway's platform combines Gen-4 and Gen-4.5 video generation, AI image generation using Runway's own and select third-party models, text-to-speech, and custom voice creation for lip sync, alongside a browser-based video editor and asset storage. Paid plans add 4K upscaling, watermark-free exports, and, at higher tiers, credit rollover and priority access to new models.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Runway uses a freemium, credit-based pricing model. The Free plan grants 125 one-time credits, while paid plans range from $12 per month (Standard, billed annually) up to $76 per month (Max, billed annually), each with a different monthly credit allowance, storage limit, and feature set. An Enterprise plan with custom credit packages and SSO is available for larger teams.

features.description: An integrated editor lets users assemble and edit AI-generated clips without leaving the platform.

features.title: Credit rollover
features.description: The Max plan allows unused monthly credits to roll over for up to one additional month.

features.title: Asset storage
features.description: Plans include tiered cloud storage for generated assets, from 5GB on Free up to 500GB on Pro.

features.title: Enterprise controls
features.description: The Enterprise plan adds SSO, workspace analytics, custom team configurations, and priority support.

pros: Gen-4 models are widely regarded as leading the field for character and scene consistency in AI video
One platform provides access to multiple leading AI models, not just Runway's own
Credit rollover on higher plans reduces waste from unused monthly allowances
Backed by roughly $860 million in funding, supporting continued model development
Integrated video editor removes the need for a separate editing tool for quick projects
Frequent model updates keep the platform competitive with fast-moving rivals
cons: Credit-based pricing can be difficult for new users to estimate against actual usage needs
Advertised monthly prices require annual billing; month-to-month costs more
Free plan output carries a watermark and offers limited storage
Higher tiers become expensive quickly for heavy, professional-scale usage
Individual video generations remain limited in length compared to traditional production tools
Enterprise pricing is not publicly listed and requires contacting sales

enrichment.short_description: An open-source, cookieless web analytics platform positioned as a simpler, privacy-friendly alternative to Google Analytics, available as a hosted service or self-hosted deployment.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium SaaS with a fully feature-complete self-hosted open-source option under AGPL-3
enrichment.starting_price: Free (3,000 events per month); paid plans from $13 per month billed annually
enrichment.company_size: Very small team, originally built and launched by a single indie developer
enrichment.seo_title: Rybbit Review 2026: Pricing and Google Analytics Alternative
enrichment.seo_meta_description: How Rybbit's cookieless, open-source web analytics platform is priced, what its self-hosted option includes, and how it compares as a Google Analytics alternative.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rybbit is an open-source, privacy-first web analytics platform designed as a simpler, cookie-free alternative to Google Analytics. It was built by indie developer Bill Yang and publicly launched on April 15, 2025 after roughly 110 days of development, growing to more than 10,000 GitHub stars within its first year, according to the project's own reporting. Rybbit's source code is licensed under AGPL-3, and the company describes remaining open source as a permanent core value rather than a temporary growth strategy. On the privacy side, Rybbit does not use cookies at all and anonymizes visitors with randomly generated display names such as Magenta Emu or Aqua Meerkat instead of tracking individual identifiers. Its hosted cloud service is run out of infrastructure located in Germany, within the European Union, which the company positions as giving it GDPR compliance by default for hosted customers. Rybbit offers three tiers on its hosted cloud plan. The Free plan includes 3,000 events per month on a single site at no cost. The Standard plan is $19 per month billed monthly, or $13 per month billed annually, and covers 100,000 events per month across up to five sites with up to three team members. The Pro plan is $39 per month billed monthly, or $26 per month billed annually, and removes the site and team member caps entirely while adding session replay and five-year data retention. Rybbit also offers a self-hosted deployment that is free under its AGPL-3 open-source license; unlike some competitors that reserve advanced functionality for paid cloud customers, Rybbit's self-hosted build is reported to be feature-complete, including session replay, funnels, and the same dashboard available to paying cloud customers. Product features across both hosted and self-hosted deployments include real-time dashboards, funnel analysis, session replay on higher tiers, and a simplified interface that reviewers describe as easier to use than Google Analytics 4 while offering more depth than minimalist privacy analytics tools like Plausible. Because it was founded in 2025 and is still run by a very small team, Rybbit is a young, fast-growing product rather than an established enterprise analytics vendor, which appeals to developers and privacy-conscious site owners who want control over their analytics stack but may carry more execution risk than legacy analytics platforms with larger support organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rybbit is an open-source, cookieless web analytics platform built as a simpler alternative to Google Analytics. It was created by indie developer Bill Yang and launched publicly in April 2025, quickly gaining traction in the open-source community and surpassing 10,000 GitHub stars within its first year. The project is licensed under AGPL-3 and is offered both as a hosted cloud service, run from EU-based infrastructure in Germany, and as a free, feature-complete self-hosted deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rybbit does not use cookies and anonymizes visitors with generated display names rather than persistent identifiers, positioning it as GDPR-friendly by default on its EU-hosted cloud plan. The platform includes real-time dashboards and funnel analysis on all tiers, with session replay and extended data retention reserved for the Pro cloud plan, though the self-hosted version includes these advanced features at no cost.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rybbit's hosted cloud service starts with a Free plan covering 3,000 events per month on one site. The Standard plan is $13 per month billed annually (or $19 monthly) for 100,000 events across up to five sites, and the Pro plan is $26 per month billed annually (or $39 monthly) with unlimited sites, unlimited team members, session replay, and five-year retention. Self-hosting the open-source version is free under the AGPL-3 license.

features.title: Cookieless tracking
features.description: Rybbit tracks site visitors without cookies, anonymizing them with generated display names instead of persistent identifiers.

features.title: Real-time dashboard
features.description: A live analytics dashboard shows visitor activity as it happens across all plans.

features.title: Funnel analysis
features.description: Built-in funnels help site owners understand conversion drop-off across a visitor journey.

features.title: Session replay
features.description: Available on the Pro cloud plan and included in the self-hosted build, session replay lets teams watch real user sessions.

features.title: EU-hosted cloud infrastructure
features.description: Rybbit's hosted service runs from Germany-based infrastructure, supporting GDPR compliance out of the box.

features.title: Feature-complete self-hosting
features.description: The free, self-hosted AGPL-3 deployment includes the same core features as paid cloud plans, including session replay and funnels.

features.title: Multi-site and multi-team support
features.description: Standard and Pro cloud plans support multiple websites and team members, with Pro removing caps entirely.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Rybbit's full source code is public and licensed under AGPL-3, with the project committed to remaining open source.

pros: Generous free tier covering 3,000 events per month without any cost
No cookies required, simplifying compliance with privacy regulations
Self-hosted version is free and feature-complete rather than a stripped-down demo
Simpler interface than Google Analytics 4 while offering more depth than minimalist alternatives
EU-hosted cloud infrastructure supports GDPR compliance by default
Affordable paid tiers relative to many analytics competitors
cons: Very young product, launched in 2025, with a shorter track record than established analytics tools
Built and maintained by a very small team, which may limit support capacity at scale
Self-hosting requires technical setup and ongoing maintenance
Advanced features on the hosted plan are gated behind the Pro tier
Smaller third-party integration ecosystem than long-established analytics platforms
Rapid early growth means the product and pricing may still change significantly

faqs.question: What is Rybbit used for
faqs.answer: Rybbit is used as a privacy-first, cookieless web analytics tool, serving as an alternative to Google Analytics for tracking site visitors and conversions.

faqs.question: Is Rybbit open source
faqs.answer: Yes, Rybbit's full codebase is open source under the AGPL-3 license, and the company states that remaining open source is a permanent core value.

faqs.question: How much does Rybbit cost
faqs.answer: Rybbit offers a free plan for up to 3,000 events per month, with paid cloud plans starting at $13 per month billed annually, or it can be self-hosted for free.

faqs.question: When was Rybbit founded
faqs.answer: Rybbit was built by indie developer Bill Yang and launched publicly on April 15, 2025.

faqs.question: Does Rybbit use cookies
faqs.answer: No, Rybbit does not use cookies and instead anonymizes visitors with generated display names.

faqs.question: Is Rybbit GDPR compliant
faqs.answer: Rybbit's hosted cloud service runs from infrastructure in Germany, within the EU, which the company positions as GDPR-friendly by default.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Rybbit for free
faqs.answer: Yes, Rybbit's self-hosted deployment is free under its AGPL-3 open-source license and is reported to include the same features as the paid cloud plans.

faqs.question: How is Rybbit different from Google Analytics
faqs.answer: Rybbit is built to be simpler and cookie-free compared to Google Analytics 4, with an emphasis on privacy and an open-source, self-hostable codebase.

enrichment.short_description: A free, open-source UPnP AV MediaServer and MediaRenderer for the GNOME desktop that shares audio, video, and photos across a home network and streams to DLNA and UPnP devices.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source, no commercial pricing
enrichment.seo_title: Rygel Guide 2026: GNOME UPnP and DLNA Media Server
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What Rygel is, how the open-source GNOME UPnP and DLNA media server works, and its key features for sharing media across a home network.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rygel is a free, open-source home media solution developed as part of the GNOME desktop ecosystem, implementing both a UPnP AV MediaServer and a MediaRenderer through a plug-in architecture. Development work on Rygel traces back to its first commit in April 2008, making it one of the longer-running components in the GNOME project family, and it is licensed under LGPL-2.1-or-later, with accompanying documentation under CC-BY-SA-3.0. Rygel is written largely in the Vala programming language and is built on top of GUPnP, GNOME's UPnP implementation library. Because it is a community-maintained open-source project under the GNOME umbrella rather than a commercial company, Rygel has no pricing, no company headquarters, and no disclosed employee count; it is maintained by volunteer and GNOME-affiliated contributors and distributed through Linux package repositories such as Fedora and via the GNOME GitLab instance. Functionally, Rygel lets a user share audio, video, and photo libraries stored on a Linux PC with other devices on the same home network, including smart TVs, games consoles such as the Sony PlayStation and Microsoft Xbox, DLNA-compatible speakers, and smartphones, without those files needing to be manually copied to each device. As a MediaRenderer, Rygel can also receive and play media pushed to it from another controller device, effectively turning a Linux machine into a networked playback target. One of Rygel's most useful capabilities is automatic transcoding: when a client device cannot natively play the format of the original media file, Rygel can transcode it on the fly into a compatible format so playback still works without the user needing to convert files manually in advance. The project ships a main server binary along with a set of plugins and shared libraries that other developers can use to build their own Rygel plugins, custom media engines, standalone renderers, or entirely separate UPnP and DLNA server implementations, reflecting its role as both an end-user application and a reusable library layer within the GNOME stack. Rygel is aimed at Linux desktop users, particularly those running GNOME, who want to stream personal media libraries to televisions, consoles, and other DLNA or UPnP-certified hardware on a local network without relying on proprietary or cloud-based media server software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rygel is a free, open-source home media server maintained as part of the GNOME project, with development dating back to its first commit in April 2008. It implements both a UPnP AV MediaServer and a MediaRenderer, letting Linux users share media libraries across a home network and stream to or from DLNA and UPnP-certified devices. The project is licensed under LGPL-2.1-or-later and written largely in the Vala programming language, built on GNOME's GUPnP library.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rygel allows users to browse and play audio, video, and photo content stored on a PC from devices like smart TVs, game consoles, and DLNA speakers elsewhere on the network. When a client device cannot play a file's original format, Rygel can automatically transcode the media on the fly so playback still succeeds. The project also ships shared libraries that developers can use to build custom Rygel plugins, alternative media engines, or standalone UPnP and DLNA server implementations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rygel is entirely free and open source under the LGPL-2.1-or-later license, distributed through Linux package repositories and the GNOME GitLab project. There is no commercial edition, subscription, or paid tier; it is community-maintained software rather than a product sold by a company.

features.description: Works with DLNA and UPnP clients including smart TVs, PlayStation and Xbox consoles, speakers, and smartphones.

features.description: Built around a plug-in mechanism that allows developers to extend or customize server behavior.

features.description: Implemented largely in the Vala language on top of GNOME's GUPnP UPnP library.

features.title: Reusable shared libraries
features.description: Ships libraries that can be used to build custom plugins, media engines, or standalone renderer and server implementations.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
Long-running, mature project with development dating back to 2008
Wide device compatibility across DLNA and UPnP standards
Automatic transcoding removes the need to manually convert media formats
Extensible plug-in architecture for developers who want custom behavior
Integrated into the GNOME desktop ecosystem for Linux users
cons: No commercial support, so users rely on community documentation and forums
Primarily targeted at Linux and GNOME users rather than cross-platform desktop users
UPnP and DLNA protocols can be finicky across different consumer device brands
Setup and troubleshooting require more technical comfort than consumer streaming apps
Development activity has varied over the years as a community-maintained project
No official mobile app; relies on third-party UPnP or DLNA client apps

faqs.answer: Rygel's first commit dates to April 2008, and it has been developed as part of the GNOME project since.

faqs.answer: Rygel is written largely in the Vala programming language, built on GNOME's GUPnP library.

enrichment.short_description: An AI writing assistant that generates marketing copy, blog content, and other short-form text across dozens of use cases, tones, and languages.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription, monthly or annual billing
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans from around $7.50 per month billed annually
enrichment.company_size: Small team, around 10 employees, operating under parent company Copysmith
enrichment.seo_title: Rytr Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Plans
enrichment.seo_meta_description: A fact-checked look at Rytr's AI writing assistant, covering its Free, Unlimited, and Premium pricing plans, features, and company background.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rytr is an AI writing assistant launched in 2021 by founders including Abhi Godara and Atul Yadav, built to generate short-form marketing copy, blog outlines, social captions, and other content across more than 40 predefined use cases. The company is based in Wilmington, Delaware, and grew to more than 2 million users before being acquired on October 6, 2022 by Copysmith, a San Francisco-based AI copywriting company that also operates its own AI content platform; after the acquisition the combined product experience was marketed under the name Copyrytr, though Rytr continued to operate as its own branded product. Rytr runs as a small team, historically reported around 10 employees, reflecting its status as a lean, product-led SaaS tool rather than a large enterprise software company. Rytr's pricing follows a freemium model with three tiers. The Free plan requires no credit card and includes 10,000 characters of AI generation per month, access to one language, no tone-of-voice matching, and no plagiarism checks, making it suitable for very light or trial use. The paid Unlimited plan is priced at roughly $9 per month billed monthly, or about $7.50 per month when billed annually with two months free, and removes the character cap entirely for unlimited generations, adding one tone-of-voice match, 50 monthly plagiarism checks, a Chrome extension, and a doubled character input limit per generation. The Premium plan costs around $29 per month billed monthly, or about $24.16 per month billed annually, and is aimed at freelancers or agencies writing for multiple brands, offering multiple tone-of-voice matches, 100 monthly plagiarism checks, access to more than 30 languages, a tripled character input limit, community access, custom use cases, and priority support. Monthly subscribers can cancel at any time, while annual plans carry a 12-month commitment in exchange for the lower effective monthly rate. Rytr's low starting price relative to many AI writing competitors has made it a frequently cited budget option for solo marketers, freelancers, and small businesses that need quick first-draft copy across many short-form use cases rather than long-form, deeply researched content, and its multi-language support extends its usefulness to non-English-speaking content creators.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rytr is an AI writing assistant launched in 2021 that generates short-form marketing and content copy across more than 40 built-in use cases. Based in Wilmington, Delaware, Rytr grew to more than 2 million users before being acquired by Copysmith in October 2022, after which the two products were marketed together under the Copyrytr name while Rytr continued as its own branded tool run by a small team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rytr covers dozens of predefined content use cases such as blog ideas, ad copy, and social captions, with adjustable tone-of-voice matching and support for more than 30 languages on its top plan. Paid plans add plagiarism checking, a Chrome browser extension, and higher per-generation character limits, while the Premium tier is built specifically for freelancers and agencies writing for multiple brands at once.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rytr's Free plan offers 10,000 characters per month with no credit card required. The Unlimited plan runs about $9 per month billed monthly, or roughly $7.50 per month billed annually, and removes the character cap. The Premium plan is about $29 per month billed monthly, or roughly $24.16 per month billed annually, and adds multi-language support, more plagiarism checks, and priority support.

features.description: Premium plan subscribers get community access and priority customer support.

cons: Free plan is limited to 10,000 characters per month and one language
Best suited to short-form content rather than long-form, deeply researched writing
Small company team size may mean slower feature development than larger AI writing competitors
Annual plans require a 12-month commitment to get the lower rate
Now operates under parent company Copysmith, which has shifted brand messaging over time
Output quality for niche or highly technical content may require heavier editing

enrichment.short_description: A free, open-source e-commerce platform built on the Laravel framework, offering multi-store, multi-language, and multi-currency online shopping cart functionality.
languages: and multi-language support built into the platform for storefronts
enrichment.seo_title: s-cart Review 2026: Open-Source Laravel E-Commerce Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What s-cart is, its key e-commerce features, technology stack, and why it is free and open source, based on the project's own documentation.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: s-cart is a free, open-source e-commerce platform built on top of the Laravel PHP framework, distributed under the broader GP247 open-source ecosystem and documented at s-cart.org. The project describes its mission as being efficient and friendly for everyone, aiming to be usable by individuals, small businesses, developers, and students who need an accessible way to run an online store without licensing costs. Technically, s-cart requires PHP 7.1.3 or higher on older releases, while its current stable branch targets PHP 8.2 and Laravel Framework 12, and the newest documented version at the time of research was 2.0.1, built on Laravel 13.x with Tailwind CSS 4 for the front end and an HMVC architecture pattern that organizes functionality into modular plugin packages such as gp247/core, gp247/front, and gp247/shop. Deployment options include both traditional server installation and Docker-based deployment, and the codebase is distributed through GitHub, Packagist, and SourceForge in addition to its own documentation site. Functionally, s-cart provides a complete shopping cart and storefront experience, including product management with support for sale price, cost price, promotional pricing, quantity tracking, multiple images, and custom attributes, along with order management, a wishlist feature, and reporting with charts, statistics, and export to CSV and PDF formats. The platform supports multi-store, multi-language, and multi-currency operation out of the box, along with multi-address customer profiles and multi-supplier functionality for marketplace-style catalogs. It also includes CMS capabilities for managing content pages, categories, and news posts alongside the storefront, an extensible plugin system for payment gateways, shipping methods, discount rules, and tax calculation, role-based admin permissions with audit logging, and a secured API layer intended for building mobile apps or third-party integrations on top of the platform. Because s-cart is a community-driven open-source project rather than a company with disclosed funding, headcount, or headquarters, there is no commercial pricing, subscription, or vendor support contract; users self-host the software on their own infrastructure and rely on the project's documentation, GitHub issues, and community channels for support, positioning s-cart as an option for technically capable teams that want full control over their e-commerce codebase rather than a managed SaaS storefront.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: s-cart is a free, open-source e-commerce platform built on the Laravel PHP framework and distributed as part of the broader GP247 open-source ecosystem. It is designed to give individuals, small businesses, developers, and students a self-hosted way to run an online store, with the project's stated mission being to stay efficient and friendly for everyone rather than gated behind licensing fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: s-cart provides a full storefront and shopping cart experience with product, order, and customer management, plus multi-store, multi-language, multi-currency, and multi-supplier support for more complex catalog needs. It includes CMS tools for pages and news, an extensible plugin system for payments, shipping, and tax, role-based admin permissions with audit logging, and a secured API for mobile or third-party integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: s-cart is entirely free and open source, with no commercial license, subscription, or vendor support fee. Users self-host the software on their own infrastructure, deploying via traditional installation or Docker, and rely on community documentation and GitHub for support rather than a paid vendor relationship.

No disclosed company or funding behind the project, so long-term maintenance depends on community contributors

faqs.answer: Yes, s-cart includes built-in multi-language and multi-currency support for running international storefronts.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source, self-hosted file sharing platform where files upload and download directly to your own S3-compatible storage rather than passing through the application server.
enrichment.seo_title: Safebucket Review 2026: Open-Source Self-Hosted File Sharing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What Safebucket is, how its direct-to-storage architecture works, and its key features for open-source, self-hosted secure file sharing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Safebucket is an open-source, self-hosted file sharing platform built around the principle that uploaded and downloaded files never pass through the application's own server. Instead, clients connect directly to a storage backend using presigned URLs, so Safebucket's server only handles metadata, access control, and audit logging rather than proxying file bytes. The project is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license and is built with a Go backend REST API and a React with TypeScript frontend, giving it a modern, developer-friendly technology stack. Because Safebucket is a community open-source project distributed through GitHub rather than a registered company with disclosed funding or headcount, there is no publicly documented founding year, company size, or headquarters; it is designed to be deployed by the organizations that use it rather than operated as a hosted SaaS product by its creators. Architecturally, every major component of Safebucket is swappable: storage can be backed by AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage, MinIO, Wasabi, Backblaze B2, DigitalOcean Spaces, Linode Object Storage, Tencent COS, Alibaba OSS, or any other S3-compatible provider; the database can be PostgreSQL or SQLite; events can flow through NATS JetStream, AWS SQS, or GCP Pub/Sub; caching can use Redis, Valkey, or an in-memory store; and activity logs can be sent to Loki or the local filesystem. Authentication is SSO-first, connecting to an organization's existing identity provider via OIDC so Safebucket does not need to manage user passwords itself. Sharing is organized around buckets with explicit membership and role-based permissions, distinguishing owners, contributors, and viewers, and the platform includes quick and reverse share links for sending files externally with configurable protections such as a password, a maximum number of downloads, and a maximum number of views. Additional features include real-time activity tracking and audit logs for compliance visibility, multifactor authentication via TOTP, and file expiration with configurable trash retention periods so deleted files are not immediately unrecoverable. Safebucket supports several deployment paths, including a stated one-minute local lite setup for quick evaluation, as well as production deployment on AWS and other cloud environments. The platform is aimed at organizations that already have their own identity provider and object storage infrastructure and want a lightweight, auditable file sharing layer on top of it, rather than businesses looking for a fully managed, vendor-hosted file sharing SaaS product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Safebucket is an open-source, self-hosted file sharing platform built so that files never pass through its own application server. Uploads and downloads connect directly to a storage backend via presigned URLs, while the Safebucket server itself only manages metadata, access control, and audit logging. It is licensed under Apache 2.0 and built with a Go backend and a React with TypeScript frontend.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Safebucket is SSO-first, authenticating users through an organization's existing OIDC identity provider rather than managing passwords itself. Sharing is organized around buckets with owner, contributor, and viewer roles, plus quick and reverse share links protected by passwords, download limits, and view limits. The platform also includes real-time activity tracking, audit logs, TOTP multifactor authentication, and configurable file expiration and trash retention.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Safebucket is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, distributed via GitHub for self-hosted deployment. There is no vendor-hosted paid tier documented; organizations deploy and run the platform on their own infrastructure, choosing their own storage, database, and event backends.

features.title: Direct-to-storage file transfer
features.description: Files upload and download directly to S3-compatible storage via presigned URLs, bypassing the application server entirely.

features.title: SSO-first authentication
features.description: Users authenticate through an organization's existing identity provider via OIDC rather than Safebucket-managed passwords.

features.title: Bucket-based access control
features.description: Sharing is organized around buckets with explicit membership and owner, contributor, and viewer roles.

features.title: Quick and reverse share links
features.description: Public share links can be protected with a password, a maximum number of downloads, and a maximum number of views.

features.title: Audit logging and activity tracking
features.description: Real-time activity logs give visibility into file access and sharing actions for compliance purposes.

features.title: Multifactor authentication
features.description: TOTP-based multifactor authentication adds an extra layer of account security.

features.title: File expiration and trash retention
features.description: Files can be set to expire automatically, with a configurable trash retention period before permanent deletion.

features.title: Swappable infrastructure components
features.description: Storage, database, events, cache, and logging backends can each be independently swapped for different providers.

pros: Files never pass through the application server, reducing server load and a potential point of exposure
Free and open source under the permissive Apache 2.0 license
Works with a wide range of S3-compatible storage providers, avoiding vendor lock-in
SSO-first design integrates cleanly with existing identity providers
Granular bucket-based roles and audit logging support compliance needs
Modern Go and React technology stack that is approachable for developers to extend
cons: Self-hosted only, requiring organizations to manage their own infrastructure and updates
No founding year, company size, or headquarters publicly disclosed, making it hard to gauge project maturity and backing
No official managed or hosted SaaS offering documented
Requires an existing OIDC identity provider and S3-compatible storage to fully set up
Smaller community and ecosystem compared to established file sharing platforms
Documentation and support rely on the open-source community rather than a dedicated vendor support team

faqs.question: What is Safebucket
faqs.answer: Safebucket is an open-source, self-hosted file sharing platform where files upload and download directly to S3-compatible storage instead of passing through the application server.

faqs.question: Is Safebucket free
faqs.answer: Yes, Safebucket is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, deployed on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: What storage providers does Safebucket support
faqs.answer: Safebucket supports AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage, MinIO, Wasabi, Backblaze B2, DigitalOcean Spaces, Linode Object Storage, Tencent COS, Alibaba OSS, and other S3-compatible providers.

faqs.question: How does Safebucket handle authentication
faqs.answer: Safebucket is SSO-first, authenticating users through an organization's existing identity provider via OIDC rather than managing passwords itself.

faqs.question: Can I password-protect shared files in Safebucket
faqs.answer: Yes, quick and reverse share links can be protected with a password, a maximum number of downloads, and a maximum number of views.

faqs.question: What technology is Safebucket built with
faqs.answer: Safebucket is built with a Go backend REST API and a React with TypeScript frontend.

faqs.question: Does Safebucket support multifactor authentication
faqs.answer: Yes, Safebucket supports TOTP-based multifactor authentication.

faqs.question: Who is Safebucket built for
faqs.answer: Safebucket is built for organizations that already have their own identity provider and object storage infrastructure and want a self-hosted, auditable file sharing layer on top of it.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team file sharing
use_cases.description: Organizations deploy Safebucket on their own infrastructure to share files internally without relying on a third-party SaaS vendor.
use_cases.audience: IT and infrastructure teams at privacy-conscious organizations

use_cases.title: Secure external file sharing
use_cases.description: Teams use Safebucket's quick and reverse share links with passwords and download limits to send files to external parties.
use_cases.audience: Teams sharing files with clients or partners

use_cases.title: Compliance-driven file access control
use_cases.description: Regulated organizations use Safebucket's bucket-based roles and audit logging to maintain accountable file access records.

features: Quick and reverse share links with password and download and view limits
Support for multiple S3-compatible storage providers
